problem_summary: 2021 MCM/ICM Problem E (sustainability). Teams were asked to re-optimize the global/national food system so that environmental sustainability and social equity become priorities alongside (not just) efficiency and profitability, then quantify the benefits and costs of the change, predict how long it takes, contrast developed vs. developing countries, and discuss scalability/adaptability. This O-award paper (control number 2102057) answers it with a three-submodel "Environment-Equity-Economy (EEE) Model" driven by two policy levers — an animal-product tax and a food-equity tax — validated on China (developing) and Finland (developed).
models_used: Logistic (sigmoid) growth ODE for diet-composition transition: dμ/dt = rμ(1−μ/μ_max), dλ/dt = rλ(1−λ/λ_max), where μ(t)=fraction of calories and λ(t)=fraction of protein from vegetal food; closed-form logistic solution used to forecast 2020–2070. Time-coefficient r controls how fast transformation completes (r=0.1→30yr China, r=0.2→20yr Finland).

Linear emission/footprint accounting model: GHE(t), EUE(t), LU(t) each = weighted sum over calories-from-veg, protein-from-veg, calories-from-animal, protein-from-animal, with per-1000kcal and per-100g intensity coefficients (carbon-dioxide-equivalent kg, phosphate-equivalent g PO4, m² land). Country-specific intensities built as convex combinations Σα_i·(intensity_i) over typical foods (rice/wheat for China; potato/wheat for Finland) and Σβ_j over animal foods (pig/poultry/beef/mutton/egg; pig/poultry/beef/fish/milk).
Exponential population/hunger growth model: N(t)=N0(1+δ)^t and baseline H(t)=H0(1+δ)^t with constant hunger ratio if no action.

Equity intervention ODE (first-order linear initial-value problem): H*'(t)/N'(t) = H0/N0 − A·t with H*(0)=H0, where A is the 'Assistance Factor'. Solved in closed form (MATLAB) to give H*(t) and the assisted population R(t)=H(t)−H*(t). Government assistance cost GA(t)=R(t)·365(x·F_cal + y·F_pro).

Benefit–cost / net-profit accounting model: costs split linearly into material, labor, land (each Σ m_food,i·p_i); profit W(t)=Σ m_food,i·p_net,i; modified net profit W_net(t)=W_veg(t)+(1−T)·W_ani(t)−GA(t), coupling the two tax levers (animal tax rate T and equity tax funding GA) back into economics.
Local sensitivity analysis: perturb food weights α_i, β_j and re-plot GHE/EUE/LU trajectories to show robustness.
key_techniques: Reframing a vague open-ended prompt as two concrete, controllable policy levers (animal-product tax T, food-equity tax/assistance factor A) so that 'sustainability' and 'equity' become tunable scalar parameters with units.

Using a single shared driver — shifting diet composition from animal to vegetal — to simultaneously move all three environmental indicators (GHG, eutrophication, land), giving one mechanism that produces three coupled benefits.
Closed-form solutions throughout (logistic solution, linear-IVP solution for H*(t)) rather than only numerical integration, making results transparent and fast to plot over 50-year horizons.

Coupling the three submodels through the economics: both the environmental tax (1−T) and the equity assistance GA(t) are subtracted in W_net, so the 'cost' of sustainability/equity is the loss in economic profit — directly answering the problem's benefit-vs-cost framing.
Country-specific parameterization via weighted-average intensity coefficients (α_i, β_j) calibrated to national diets, letting the same model instantiate for China and Finland.

Two contrasting case studies chosen deliberately to span the developed/developing and large/small axes the problem asked about (1.4B vs 5.5M population, 250× scale difference) — turning case selection into evidence for scalability.

Quantified, dated deliverables (e.g., China −25.79% GHG / no hunger by 2029 / −5.14% profit; Finland −33.22% GHG / no hunger by mid-2027 / −9.77% profit) that map one-to-one onto the problem's questions.
why_it_won: From a judge's perspective this paper wins on completeness of mapping to the prompt and on interpretability, not on mathematical sophistication. Every sub-question of Problem E is answered with a concrete number and date: it re-prioritizes the four factors via explicit policy levers, gives benefits AND costs in the same currency (percentage profit loss vs. percent emission cut), predicts time-to-achieve (logistic r and assistance factor A set the clock), explicitly contrasts developed vs developing countries, and tests scalability with a 250×-scale-spanning pair of real countries. The EEE naming/structure is memorable and the whole model reduces to a small set of named, unit-bearing parameters (T, A, μ, λ, r, δ) a judge can audit on one page. The logistic 'transition takes decades' framing is intuitively correct and matches common sense, and the sensitivity analysis closes the loop on robustness. It is a clean, self-consistent, end-to-end answer — judges reward a paper that visibly hits every rubric item over one that is deeper but partial.
section_highlights: Strong structural choices a future team can copy: (1) A one-page Approach flowchart (Figure 2) plus an "EEE" mnemonic that makes the three-pillar architecture instantly graspable. (2) A Basic-Idea section (Sec 4) that states the thesis — current system over-prioritizes economics, we add Environment + Equity — before any math, so the reader knows the destination. (3) Each submodel follows the same template: motivation → indicator selection → general formula → country-specific parameter table → result plot, which makes 20+ pages navigable. (4) Twin case-study sections (China then Finland) with identical sub-structure and matching summary tables (Tables 3 & 5) let the judge compare developed vs developing at a glance. (5) Results are always delivered as quantified, dated bullet lists and consequence tables rather than buried in prose. (6) Explicit, individually-justified Assumptions section and a Notation table up front. (7) A genuine Strengths/Weaknesses/Future-Work section that honestly names the model's limits (semi-educated-guess parameters, only calories+protein, breaks in extremely poor/rich regions) — candor judges reward.
pitfalls_or_limitations: Data integrity is weak: nearly all parameters come from one source (Our World in Data), several are openly called 'semi-educated guess,' and the result figures appear schematic/illustrative rather than computed from a reproducible pipeline — a rigor-focused judge would dock this.
Internal inconsistency in parameters: the China constants table lists time coefficient r=0.1 but the text says r=0.01 for a 30-year transition; such slips undermine reproducibility.

The equity ODE H*'(t)/N'(t)=H0/N0 − A·t is ad hoc — the linear-in-t assistance term has no mechanistic justification and lets hunger be driven to exactly zero by construction, which makes the 'no hunger by 2029/2027' headline somewhat circular.

Economics is shallow: prices assumed constant (Assumption 2), no supply-demand response, no elasticity, so the animal tax changes production by assumption rather than through a market model; the headline 'only −5%/−10% profit' isn't stress-tested against behavioral response.
Only calories and protein modeled; micronutrients, food waste dynamics, and trade/transport emissions explicitly neglected — acknowledged but limits real-world validity.

Sensitivity analysis is narrow: only food-weight perturbations (α_i, β_j) are tested; the high-leverage parameters (r, A, T, δ, μ_max/λ_max) — which actually drive the headline numbers — are not varied, so 'robustness' is overstated.
Numerous spelling/typographical errors and OCR-garbled equations in the text reflect rushed proofreading.
Hunger-ratio baselines (China 1%, Finland 2%) are taken at face value and the claim that Finland's hunger is 'worse than China' is asserted without scrutiny.
reusable_patterns: Policy-lever pattern: convert a fuzzy objective (sustainability, equity) into one or two scalar control parameters with units (a tax rate T∈[0,1], an assistance factor A) and let the model's outputs be functions of them — instantly answers 'how do we re-prioritize' and 'what's the cost/benefit tradeoff.'

Logistic-transition pattern: when a system must shift from state A to state B over years/decades, model the share variable with a logistic ODE and expose the rate constant r as 'time to achieve' — directly answers MCM 'how long will it take' sub-questions.

Shared-driver, multiple-indicators pattern: find one mechanism (diet shift) that moves several required metrics at once, then express each metric as the same weighted-sum accounting formula with swappable intensity coefficients.
Closed-form-first pattern: solve simple ODEs analytically so 50-year forecasts are transparent and one-line to plot, reserving MATLAB only for the messier linear IVP.
Coupling-through-cost pattern: make the 'price' of every soft objective show up in a single economic ledger (W_net), so benefits and costs are reported in commensurable terms.

Span-the-axes case-study pattern: pick exactly two real instances chosen to bracket the dimensions the prompt cares about (developed/developing, large/small) so case selection itself becomes evidence for scalability/adaptability.

Mnemonic-architecture pattern: give the model a short memorable name (EEE) with one submodel per letter and a single front-page flowchart, plus identical templated sub-structure per submodel and per case study.

Quantified-dated-deliverable pattern: end every analysis with a bullet list / summary table of signed percentages and calendar years that map one-to-one onto the problem's questions, and pair it with an honest Strengths/Weaknesses section.
